coredata.dc:title: Phase equilibria of the Cu–Nb–Ti system at 850°C
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Honghui
Du, Yong
Huang, Baiyun
Liu, Shuhong
coredata.dc:description: The phase equilibria of the Cu–Nb–Ti system at 850°C were investigated by a high-efficiency diffusion couple approach. The samples were studied by means of optical microscopy, scanning electron microscopy and electron probe microanalysis. At 850°C, the phase TiCu2, the stability range of which is controversial in the literature, was observed. The solubilities of Nb in Ti2Cu, TiCu and Ti3Cu4 were determined to be 3.5, 3.1 and 4.6 at.%, respectively. The solubility of Nb in TiCu4 was negligibly small. The following three-phase equilibria were observed: Ti2Cu + TiCu + (Nb,Ti), TiCu + Ti3Cu4 + (Nb,Ti) and Ti3Cu4 + TiCu4 + (Nb,Ti).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu–Nb–Ti ternary system
Phase equilibrium
Diffusion couple
EPMA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Microstructure of the Cu–Nb–Ti couple annealed at 850°C for 588h: (a) backscattered electron (BSE) image of a Cu–Nb–Ti couple and (b) local magnification image for the zone A in (a).
BSE images of the NbTi alloy/Cu diffusion couples annealed at 850°C for 588h: (a) diffusion couple Nb10Ti90/Cu, (b) diffusion couple Nb20Ti80/Cu and (c) diffusion couple Nb35Ti65/Cu.
Isothermal section of the Cu–Nb–Ti system at 850°C.
XRD patterns of the alloys annealed at 850°C for 240h: (a) alloy Cu50.0Nb6.0Ti44.0 and (b) alloy Cu60.0Nb12.0Ti28.0.
The nominal compositions of three Nb–Ti binary alloys used to prepare the NbTi alloy/Cu diffusion couples (in at.%)
The tie-line data on the phase equilibria of the Cu–Nb–Ti system at 850°C (in at.%)
The tie-triangle data on the phase equilibria of the Cu–Nb–Ti system at 850°C (in at.%)

article.ce:title: Phase equilibria of the Cu–Nb–Ti system at 850°C

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The phase equilibria of the Cu–Nb–Ti system at 850°C were investigated by a high-efficiency diffusion couple approach. The samples were studied by means of optical microscopy, scanning electron microscopy and electron probe microanalysis. At 850°C, the phase TiCu2, the stability range of which is controversial in the literature, was observed. The solubilities of Nb in Ti2Cu, TiCu and Ti3Cu4 were determined to be 3.5, 3.1 and 4.6 at.%, respectively. The solubility of Nb in TiCu4 was negligibly small. The following three-phase equilibria were observed: Ti2Cu + TiCu + (Nb,Ti), TiCu + Ti3Cu4 + (Nb,Ti) and Ti3Cu4 + TiCu4 + (Nb,Ti).

ce:section.ce:para: The Cu–Nb composites [1] and the Cu–Ti alloys [2] show great promise for applications as high-conductivity and high-strength materials. Knowledge of phase diagram of the Cu–Nb–Ti system is of importance to the development of this class of the high-conductivity and high-strength materials. Up to now, no information on the phase equilibria of the Cu–Nb–Ti ternary system is available. The objective of the present study is to investigate the phase equilibria of the Cu–Nb–Ti system at 850°C via a high-efficiency diffusion couple approach.

Kumar et al. [3] and Canale and Servant [4], thermodynamically evaluated the Cu–Ti system, respectively. There are six intermediate phases in the Cu–Ti system, i.e., TiCu4, TiCu2, Ti2Cu3, Ti3Cu4, TiCu and Ti2Cu. The existence of the phase Ti3Cu, and the stability range of the phase TiCu2 is controversial. Canale and Servant [4] suggested that the phase Ti3Cu is a stable phase on the basis of their experimental results though it is generally regarded as a metastable phase [3,5]. Eremenko et al. [6] reported that the temperature for the invariant reaction TiCu2 ⇔TiCu4 +Ti2Cu3 was 850°C. However, this temperature suggested by Murray [5] was about 870°C. The Nb–Ti system [7] and the Cu–Nb system [8] are simple systems without any intermediate compound.

Copper (99.9wt.%), niobium (99.8wt.%) and titanium (99.9wt.%) were used as the starting materials. The binary Nb–Ti couples were first prepared by diffusion welding the elemental bars in a silica capsule back-filled with high purity argon at 1100°C for 72h. One of the Nb–Ti couples and a Cu block were ground, polished, cleaned and then swathed together with Mo wires to make a Cu–Nb–Ti diffusion couple, which was wrapped in Cu foil. In order to prepare the NbTi alloy/Cu diffusion couples, three Nb–Ti binary alloys listed in Table 1 were prepared by arc melting pure elements in an arc furnace under argon atmosphere using a non-consumable tungsten electrode. The buttons were remelted three times to improve their homogeneity. From the buttons slices of approximate dimensions of 4mm×4mm×10mm were cut. The NbTi alloy/Cu diffusion couples were prepared with the same method as above-mentioned. The NbTi alloy/Cu and Cu–Nb–Ti diffusion couples, which were sealed in a silica capsule back-filled with high purity argon, were annealed in an L4514-type diffusion furnace (Qingdao Instrument & Equipment Co. Ltd., China) at 850°C for 588h and subsequently quenched in water.

After standard metallographic preparation, the Cu–Nb–Ti couples were studied first using optical microscopy (model: Leica DMLP, Germany) and then using SEM (model: JSM-5600LV, JEOL, Japan). The phase equilibria in the couples were determined via electron probe microanalysis (EPMA) (model: JXA-8800R, JEOL, Japan).

Two ternary alloys, Cu50.0Nb6.0Ti44.0 and Cu60.0Nb12.0Ti28.0 (in at.%), were prepared by arc melting, and remelted three times to improve their homogeneity. The alloys’ samples were annealed at 850°C for 240h for the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis to confirm some of the results obtained by EPMA from the Cu–Nb–Ti diffusion couples.

The microstructure of the Cu–Nb–Ti couple annealed at 850°C for 588h is shown in Fig. 1 . The microstructures of the NbTi alloy/Cu diffusion couples annealed at 850°C for 588h are presented in Fig. 2 .

As shown in Fig. 1, the phase Ti3Cu, which was reported by Canale and Servant [4], was not observed, that is, in accord with Kumar et al. [3] and Murray [5]. According to Eremenko et al. [6], TiCu2 decomposes eutectoidally at 850°C, whereas the eutectoid temperature suggested by Murray [5] was about 870°C. In the present study, the annealing temperature was 850°C and the phase TiCu2 was observed, which suggested that the temperature for the invariant reaction TiCu2 ⇔TiCu4 +Ti2Cu3 should not be higher than 850°C.

In the diffusion couple Nb10Ti90/Cu, as can be seen in Fig. 2(a), the phases TiCu4, Ti3Cu4, TiCu and Ti2Cu occurred. The three-phase conjunction interfaces respectively corresponding to Ti2Cu + TiCu + (Nb,Ti), TiCu + Ti3Cu4 + (Nb,Ti) and Ti3Cu4 + TiCu4 + (Nb,Ti) were observed. In the diffusion couples Nb20Ti80/Cu and Nb35Ti65/Cu, as can be seen in Fig. 2(b and c), there appeared a large region where the phase (Nb,Ti) was dentritically distributed.

The tie-line data and the tie-triangle data obtained with EPMA from the Cu–Nb–Ti diffusion couples are listed in Tables 2 and 3
, respectively. Using these experimental data, the isothermal section at 850°C was constructed in Fig. 3 .

The solubilities of Nb in Ti2Cu, TiCu and Ti3Cu4 were determined to be 3.5, 3.1 and 4.6 at.%, respectively. The solubility of Nb in TiCu4 was negligibly small. High solubility of Cu in (Nb,Ti) was observed up to the Nb-rich corner.

Fig. 4(a and b) presents the XRD patterns corresponding to the alloys Cu50.0Nb6.0Ti44.0 and Cu60.0Nb12.0Ti28.0, respectively. The XRD results suggest that at 850°C, the alloys Cu50.0Nb6.0Ti44.0 and Cu60.0Nb12.0Ti28.0 are located in the three-phase equilibrium regions TiCu + Ti3Cu4 + (Nb,Ti) and Ti3Cu4 + TiCu4 + (Nb,Ti), respectively, which is in accord with the results obtained by EPMA from the Cu–Nb–Ti diffusion couples.
The phase equilibria of the Cu–Nb–Ti system at 850°C were investigated by a high-efficiency diffusion couple approach.

At 850°C, the phase TiCu2 was observed. The solubilities of Nb in Ti2Cu, TiCu and Ti3Cu4 were determined to be 3.5, 3.1 and 4.6 at.%, respectively. The solubility of Nb in TiCu4 was negligibly small. High solubility of Cu in (Nb,Ti) was observed up to the Nb-rich corner. The following three-phase equilibria were observed: Ti2Cu + TiCu + (Nb,Ti), TiCu + Ti3Cu4 + (Nb,Ti) and Ti3Cu4 + TiCu4 + (Nb,Ti).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Outstanding Youth Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 50425103) is greatly acknowledged. One of the authors (Yong Du) acknowledges Furong Chair Professorship Program released by Hunan Province of PR China for financial support. The donation of Leica DMLP microscope from Alexander von Humboldt Foundation is greatly appreciated.
